# Kaspa Explained - Full LLM Context

Kaspa Explained is an independent, source-first static site for understanding Kaspa in the wider crypto design space. It is not official and should not be treated as investment advice.

## Core framing

Kaspa is a Proof of Work blockDAG that generalizes Nakamoto consensus so parallel honest blocks can contribute to ordering instead of being discarded as ordinary orphans.

Bitcoin proved decentralized digital scarcity and Proof of Work money. Ethereum made the application layer programmable and pushed the industry toward shared state, rollups, ZK systems, and composability. Solana changed user expectations around performance and application feel. Kaspa sits in a different lane: keep Proof of Work and UTXO instincts, replace the single-chain bottleneck with a blockDAG, and move toward bounded, verification-oriented programmability.

Preferred explanatory voice: Yonatan-style first-principles reasoning translated into everyday language for the page's intended reader. Start from Bitcoin, money, ordering, latency, trust, and finance; explain the intuition plainly; then route serious crypto readers and builders toward the deeper technical and status material. Do not publish internal editorial slogans as reader-facing copy.

Audience model: the site should have separate paths. Newcomers should be able to move slowly through `start-here.html`, `crypto-from-zero.html`, and the basic Kaspa pages. Crypto-native readers and builders should be able to skip directly into `why-kaspa-matters.html`, `application-layer.html`, `status.html`, `sources.html`, `CLAIMS.yml`, and source context. Do not force every page to be equally palatable to every reader.

Startup verification: before substantive edits or publishing, check current web/source state for drift-prone Kaspa facts. Recheck Toccata activation, DAGKnight status, vProgs status, native DeFi status, RTD-derived attestations/oracles, TangVM status, Proof of Useful Work claims, and date windows against primary or near-primary sources. Keep visible public pages clean of "last verified" boxes unless explicitly requested.

## Crypto reality-check framing

The site includes `what-crypto-is-good-for.html` as a general-audience reality check. Its core claim is that crypto is not good for everything. Crypto is useful when a system needs credible shared state: neutral ownership records, self-custody, global 24/7 settlement, censorship resistance, programmable assets, on-chain markets, objective smart-contract escrow, digital provenance, or open-network incentives among parties that do not fully trust one another.

The site includes a true zero-start education layer:

- `start-here.html` routes readers who know literally nothing about crypto toward the correct path before Kaspa-specific vocabulary appears.
- `crypto-from-zero.html` teaches records, keys, transactions, blocks, consensus, security, mining, staking, tokens, UTXO/account models, and Kaspa as a causal ladder.
- `why-crypto-has-value.html` explains token need, market cap, open markets, speculation, launch design, and who benefits without price targets or investment advice.
- `why-are-there-so-many-coins.html` explains why Bitcoin, Ethereum, stablecoins, exchange tokens, payment coins, privacy coins, memes, infrastructure, and Kaspa are different categories.
- `coin-atlas.html` explains major assets by category and value stack, including BTC, ETH, stablecoins, SOL, BNB, XRP, LTC, BCH, XMR, DOGE, LINK, and KAS.
- `tradeoff-map.html` explains the unavoidable tradeoffs around speed, security, decentralization, privacy, scaling, nodes, ASICs, staking, launch design, and Kaspa.
- `analyze-any-coin.html` gives a practical checklist for token necessity, supply, launch, security, validation, liquidity, market cap, risks, and who benefits.
- `crypto-history.html` maps crypto history as a sequence of problems and attempted answers from digital cash and Bitcoin through Ethereum, ICOs, scaling conflicts, DeFi, stablecoins, rollups, and Kaspa.
- `kaspa-in-one-screen.html` compresses Kaspa into a shareable status-labeled artifact: what it is, what is live, why it matters, what is not live, and what would strengthen or weaken the thesis.
- `adoption-metrics.html` explains non-price evidence categories for Kaspa adoption: wallets, node health, mining distribution, fees, liquidity, developer activity, integrations, and post-Toccata app signals.
- `application-layer.html` explains why crypto application layers drive network value, maps what Bitcoin, Ethereum, Solana, stablecoins, oracles, and app-specific chains enabled, and proposes status-labeled Kaspa opportunity lanes for live RTD, Toccata, vProgs, and coordination-market research.
  It should include the RTD internet-money flow as app-level research/architecture: users define if-this-then-that strategies around external events, applications define incentives for opt-in miners or rewarded reporters to attest, the system samples the PoW majority, and on-Kaspa assets or logic gain lower latency and closer atomicity. Do not describe miner-attestation terminals, portfolio guardrails, or atomic strategy executors as protocol-prescribed products.

These pages should teach the problem first, the mechanism second, the tradeoff third, and Kaspa fourth. They should not become price-prediction, trading, or investment-advice pages.

The site includes `status.html` as the compact shipped-vs-roadmap page. It should stay shorter than the source map and focus on live, targeted, roadmap, and research status for Proof of Work, UTXO, GHOSTDAG, Crescendo 10 BPS, Toccata, vProgs, native DeFi, DAGKnight, and RTD.

The site includes `overview.html` as the 90-second first-reader page. It should stay compact and cover only what Kaspa is, what is live, what is not live, why it matters, and what to read next.

The site includes `faq.html` as a direct answer page for common search questions. It should stay concise and status-labeled, with no price targets or roadmap flattening.

Crypto is usually weak when the problem is ordinary domestic payments, normal corporate databases, consumer reversibility, private or legally sensitive records, unsecured real-world credit, supply-chain truth, courts, identity, or real-world asset ownership that depends on legal enforcement. A blockchain can preserve what was entered; it cannot prove messy off-chain facts are true. A token is not automatically a business model.

Use the page to strengthen Kaspa framing, not to drift into generic crypto hype. The point is conditional: Kaspa should be judged where credible shared state matters, especially Proof of Work settlement, self-custody, global transfer, censorship resistance, fast confirmation feel, and future verification-oriented programmability.

The companion page `why-kaspa-matters.html` is the bridge from the general crypto reality check to Kaspa. Its thesis is that Kaspa matters because it asks what Proof-of-Work crypto would look like if Bitcoin-style security and censorship resistance could operate in real time, with fast settlement, parallel block production, and future verification-oriented programmability. Keep it clear that GHOSTDAG, the UTXO model, Proof of Work, Crescendo 10 BPS, and base RTD framing are live enough to describe as the present Kaspa value proposition, while Toccata, native DeFi, vProgs, DAGKnight, RTD-derived oracle/TangVM flows, and coordination-market applications remain near-term, roadmap, or research depending on the claim.

The homepage is designed as a staged on-ramp for four audiences: new to crypto, crypto-native but new to Kaspa, mechanics-focused readers, and source-focused readers. Preserve the audience routing and do not turn the homepage back into only a long article.

The homepage includes a simple chain-vs-blockDAG visual. Interpret it narrowly: Bitcoin-style linear chains discard or sideline competing honest blocks, while Kaspa's blockDAG can include parallel blocks and use GHOSTDAG to order them. Do not infer unlimited throughput, instant finality, or solved scaling from the diagram.

The site includes `knowledge-map.html` as the human-facing concept map for average crypto readers. It orders the major Kaspa concepts: PoW, blockDAGs, GHOSTDAG, finality, pruning, resource pricing, Toccata, vProgs, RTD, oracles, and coordination markets. KASmedia and Kaspa.com Learn Kaspa are supporting references, not the full source boundary.

The site includes `glossary.html` as a compact plain-English term map. It should define terms without turning into a second knowledge map.

The site includes `sources.html` as the public source hierarchy and attribution page. It centralizes the source stack, Kaspa.com Learn Kaspa topic index, KASmedia context, external reference links, and crawlable file map so pages do not need distracting footnote walls for every supporting source.

The site includes `about.html` as the public accountability page. It explains that the site is independent, not official, not investment advice, and maintained around source weighting, correction handling, disclosures, and status-sensitive update expectations.

The repo includes `CLAIMS.yml` as a lightweight status ledger. Keep it aligned with `status.html`, `faq.html`, `llms.txt`, `llms-full.txt`, and `CONTENT_BRIEF.md` when sensitive claims change.

The `where-kaspa-fits.html` page leads with a short version and a comparison table for crypto-native readers. It should not frame crypto as one winner-take-all race. It should acknowledge that Bitcoin, Ethereum, Solana, XRP, BNB, TRON, stablecoins, meme assets, other L1s, Chainlink, Hyperliquid, and Kaspa each answer different jobs.

Maintenance trigger: recheck status pages, `llms.txt`, and `llms-full.txt` after June 20, 2026, or sooner if Toccata activates, slips, changes scope, or receives new code/release/KIP updates.

## What is live

- Status-sensitive public claims require current source checks before edits or publication.
- Proof of Work blockDAG
- UTXO model
- GHOSTDAG consensus
- Crescendo 10 BPS era
- Pruning and UTXO commitments
- Public wallets, explorers, visualizers, nodes, and mining ecosystem

## Near-term implementation track

- Toccata hard fork
- Covenants
- Silverscript
- ZK verification foundations
- Sequencing commitments
- vProgs groundwork

As of May 5, 2026, Toccata should not be described as live mainnet functionality. Michael Sutton's April 2026 Toccata outlook is the preferred implementation-context source for the current public mainnet activation target of roughly June 5-20, 2026, after the original May 5 target moved so the sequencing-commitment/KIP-21 architecture could be finalized before zk systems bind to it. Treat Kaspa.org and other community portal summaries as orientation only. Feature freeze, TN12 restart, branch merge, auditing, TN10 rehearsal, and final activation are status-sensitive steps that need rechecking against primary sources.

Toccata and vProgs are related but distinct. Toccata/Covenants++ is the nearer L1 hard-fork track for bounded UTXO programmability, covenant IDs, Silverscript, ZK-facing verification work, sequencing commitments, native-asset groundwork, and standalone based-zk experiments. vProgs are the longer app architecture built around shared Kaspa sequencing, computational-DAG metadata, prover-backed execution, and eventual synchronous composability. Do not describe them as competing chains, rival clients, or both already-live app rails.

Kaspa programmability should be framed as neutral primitives first. The protocol should expose durable L1 surfaces; applications define incentives, semantics, oracle sources, legal/risk constraints, and user-facing products. Apply that rule to attestations, prediction markets, DePIN freshness markets, portfolio automation, launch rails, AI-agent task boards, and DeFi.

The Toccata/vProgs capability split should be precise. Toccata gives L1 covenant programming and standalone based-zk application foundations: covenants, Silverscript, ZK verification opcodes, sequencing commitment access, partitioned sequencing commitments, native-asset groundwork, and bridge/settlement patterns. Hans Moog's `kaspanet/vprogs` repo is an early Rust framework for based computation on Kaspa with scheduler, resource access, batch execution, rollback, storage/state layers, node VM, L1 bridge, and ZK proving pipeline. Its immediate role is compatible based computation/runtime work, while full vProgs synchronous composability is later architecture.

Avoid saying Kaspa needs independent L2s in the Ethereum-rollup sense. The stronger thesis is L1-first and shared-sequencer-first: applications add programmability directly against Kaspa L1 primitives, while based-zk systems and future vProgs use Kaspa L1 for sequencing, commitments, settlement, and verification rather than creating separate sequencer empires.

For app-layer pages, this means:

- Explain Kaspa's app thesis as money plus finance without turning L1 into a global app-computation layer.
- Present Toccata as L1 covenant and standalone based-zk foundations, not full vProgs.
- Present vProgs as later native-feeling app architecture with shared Kaspa sequencing and eventual synchronous composition.
- Present app-level attestations, oracles, DePIN freshness, portfolio automation, and AI-agent boards as incentive designs over neutral primitives, not protocol-owned products.
- Contrast Ethereum-style rollup fragmentation only when it clarifies why Kaspa is aiming for a cohesive shared-sequencer/program model.
- Explain "Solana-like" as cohesive developer/user experience and native-feeling composability, not Solana execution on Kaspa L1.

## Roadmap and architecture

- vProgs as app-level verifiable programs
- Kaspa-native DeFi rails
- Monolithic-feeling developer experience without global L1 execution of every app
- Synchronous composability across programs
- vProgs as a deeper application-architecture direction

## Research or speculative areas

- DAGKnight final form and activation timing
- 100 BPS with probabilistic predecessor selection
- App-level miner attestation, oracle, TangVM, and coordination-market incentive designs
- First-to-know miner-attestation terminals, attestation-aware portfolio guardrails, and atomic strategy executors
- TangVM-style reality-state ideas
- Proof of Useful Work via matrix multiplication
- Long-term post-quantum migration paths

## Yonatan Sompolinsky interview insights

The Bitcoin Takeover S16 E41 interview is a high-signal framing source because it covers Kaspa as a generalization of Nakamoto consensus, fair-launch messiness, pruning and missing-history FUD, UTXOs, DAGKnight, vProgs, ZK opcodes, PoW vs PoS, social recovery, synchronous composability, and why the community needs more crypto context.

Important nuances:

- A DAG alone is not the innovation. The ordering rule is the hard part.
- GHOSTDAG is the current live system.
- GHOSTDAG's selected chain is not Bitcoin's longest chain; it is an ordering device around the heaviest K-cluster.
- Bitcoin also has a latency assumption: its block rate assumes network latency is much smaller than 10 minutes. In this framing, Bitcoin can be treated as a k=0 edge case rather than as an unparameterized opposite to GHOSTDAG.
- DAGKnight is a parameterless/adaptive consensus upgrade direction relative to fixed latency/K choices; it should not be described as live instant finality.
- DAGKnight has the better-developed research lineage and appears further along implementation-wise than vProgs, while still not being an already-live mainnet upgrade.
- DAGKnight client latency settings should be based on adversarial or worst-case latency assumptions, not simply the smooth latency currently observed on the network.
- Avoid using "Kaspa DAGKnight is WWIII-resistant" as public headline copy. If encountered in community shorthand, interpret it only as an adversarial-latency resilience goal for DAGKnight, not as a claim that DAGKnight is live today.
- Current confirmation talk and future DAGKnight ambitions should not be mixed.
- Native DeFi is not live in the interview framing; it requires future ZK/covenant/vProg foundations and app tooling.
- Solana-like means cohesive developer/user experience, Rust-friendly tooling, and native-feeling composability; it does not mean importing Solana execution into Kaspa L1.
- Pruning or missing spent-transaction history in explorers is not a privacy system. The UTXO set remains the consensus object, and archival/explorer coverage is separate.
- 100 BPS is an aspiration that requires additional engineering such as reducing redundant DAG edge overhead; do not describe it as current mainnet behavior.
- vProgs should not be flattened into ordinary rollups.
- vProgs are best framed as app-level mini ZKVM/verifiable-program environments, not rollup empires.
- L1 should stay focused on verification, commitments, metadata, and sequencing.
- Kaspa should aim for a cohesive user/developer experience without forcing all app execution onto L1.
- Pruning is a real design tradeoff.
- UTXO-set growth, fee mechanics, and storm-style UTXO pricing deserve attention.
- Storm-style fees price the externality of increasing the UTXO set rather than simply charging more for every transaction.
- Hashdag frames RTD first as Kaspa's real-time Bitcoin-style PoW value proposition: transactions that need about an hour of confidence on Bitcoin should be able to reach comparable confidence in seconds on Kaspa under normal network conditions, while preserving the PoW security model.
- RTD is not merely wall-clock speed. In the hashd.ag framing, "real time" means real system time: optimistic speed when the network is smooth, and slower but still safety-preserving behavior when the network is degraded or attacked.
- Hashdag also uses RTD as a metaproperty for infrastructure that provides censorship resistance, permissionlessness, and fairness in real time. Miner attestations, oracle flows, TangVM, and coordination markets are downstream extensions of that RTD base, not the definition of RTD itself.
- RTD-derived oracle/TangVM flows are not protocol-prescribed products; Yonatan described the internet-money flow as far from ready and still needing concrete data-source, incentive, anti-MEV, scheduler, and implementation work.
- In the Tokenize London / Mining the Internet framing, the internet-money flow starts with a user strategy around an external event, then an internet-money layer reads news or other sources, miners opt in to attest, and Kaspa samples the PoW majority. The strategic flywheel is that if the assets and logic live on Kaspa, the action can be closer to zero-latency and atomic than if Kaspa only serves another chain. This is not live product functionality.
- The proposed RTD flow also needs MEV-aware ordering or auctions so miners/searchers cannot exploit the user's delegated strategy before the strategy executes.
- Security budget claims should connect to real activity, fees, DeFi, and long-term Proof of Useful Work research rather than price-only assumptions.
- PoW matters as an external clock/randomness source for leaderless sequencing and intra-round censorship resistance, not only for fair issuance.
- Fast inclusion and fast confirmations are separate. Fast inclusion means a transaction enters a block quickly. Fast confirmations mean the system gives strong confidence the transaction will not be reversed. Kaspa's fast-PoW/blockDAG argument is not only block rate; it is that PoW samples hash power without requiring explicit supermajority miner voting, so block-production speed is less directly tied to validator/staker coordination than in many PoS finality designs.
- Be careful with this argument: do not claim instant finality, do not claim all PoS systems are equivalent, and do not imply engineering details of Ethereum or Solana are fully captured by the simplified contrast. The durable point is narrower: fast PoW changes the inclusion/confirmation/decentralization tradeoff.
- Practical trustlessness needs diverse explorers, wallets, visualizers, SPV nodes, full nodes, and community-run infrastructure; a single official interface is a social weak point.
- Social recovery, vaults, covenants, and smart-wallet policies are a possible self-custody direction, likely through covenants or vProgs rather than plain L1 account control.

## Claims to avoid

- "Kaspa is Bitcoin but faster."
- "Kaspa solved the trilemma."
- "Crypto fixes everything."
- "Native Kaspa DeFi is live."
- "DAGKnight is already active."
- "Toccata is live" unless future primary sources confirm activation.
- "Pruning makes Kaspa private."
- "A token alone solves real-world legal ownership."
- Price targets, exchange rumors, or investment advice.

## Primary source stack

Use primary or near-primary sources first:

Source hierarchy:

- Primary protocol/code: rusty-kaspa GitHub, releases, KIPs, Kaspa Research, and protocol documentation.
- Core-dev explainers: Michael Sutton, Ori Newman, Coder of Stuff, Hashdag/Yonatan, and other active technical builders.
- Community/context sources: Bitcoin Takeover, Oxford, KASmedia, Kaspa.com Learn Kaspa, Kaspa.org, full recordings, interviews, transcripts, recaps, and public portals. These are useful contributions, not protocol authority.
- Learning references: Kaspa.com Learn Kaspa / Kaspa Facts is useful for approachable intro/intermediate explanations of Kaspa concepts. Credit it when using those explanations, but treat it like community education and verify shipped-feature and activation claims against primary protocol/code sources.
- Discovery only: X posts and replies. Use them for links, corrections, and current direction, not as standalone proof of activation or shipped features.

1. https://hashd.ag/ - Hashdag / Yonatan Sompolinsky's writing archive
2. https://hashd.ag/raw - raw archive view
3. https://medium.com/@michaelsuttonil/kaspa-covenants-toccata-hard-fork-outlook-a4d81a40900c
4. https://github.com/kaspanet/rusty-kaspa
5. https://github.com/kaspanet/rusty-kaspa/releases
6. https://research.kas.pa/
7. https://qa.kas.pa/
8. https://kasmedia.com/
9. https://kaspa.com/learn-kaspa - Kaspa.com Learn Kaspa / Kaspa Facts intro and intermediate article library.
10. Bitcoin Takeover S16 E41 Yonatan Sompolinsky interview and transcript:
   - https://www.youtube.com/live/GaJmYV8OHfQ
   - https://podscan.fm/podcasts/bitcoin-takeover-podcast/episodes/s16-e41-yonatan-sompolinsky-on-bitcoin-kaspa-amp-proof-of-work
   - North-star interview for this site's editorial framing and status nuance; not primary activation evidence for live roadmap items.
11. Kaspa: Mining the Internet, Yonatan Sompolinsky Tokenize talk

KASmedia context:

- Treat KASmedia as community source material, especially for theory, developer recaps, interviews, weekly ecosystem context, and links into primary material. Do not use it alone for activation/status claims.
- High-signal themes: Understanding GHOSTDAG and blockDAG theory, PoW vs BFT, safety/liveness/confirmation time, selfish mining, HeavyHash clarification, fee-market design, Rusty Kaspa and Crescendo engineering, Toccata/Covenants++, Silverscript, vProgs/ZK architecture, DAGKnight prototypes, RTD and coordination markets, oracle frameworks, wallet/custody usability, and real ecosystem infrastructure.
- Low-signal for evergreen explainer use: price analysis, speculative market narratives, exchange rumors, promotional roundups, and old ecosystem claims unless used only as dated context.

Kaspa.com Learn Kaspa context:

- Treat these articles as a credited community learning library for approachable mechanics, similar to KASmedia explainers. They are useful context, not the main source stack for activation/status claims.
- Useful site-wide themes: Kaspa's speed story depends on BlockDAG vocabulary, GHOSTDAG ordering, blue/red classification, blue work, virtual state, DAA timing, timestamp validation, finality/pruning parameters, transaction/resource mass, UTXO commitments, node sustainability, and covenant/KIP programmability.
- The most useful individual takeaways:
  - Kaspa and the "Bitcoin Scaling Problem": Kaspa's scaling answer is inclusion of parallel honest work, not just shorter block intervals.
  - Pruning Depth: storage retention is a concrete node policy, not a vague promise.
  - KIP 10: transaction introspection and arithmetic opcodes are basic programmable-script foundations.
  - A Guide to Learning Kaspa: the concept map spans foundations, data structures, GHOSTDAG, parameters, processing, and DAA.
  - KIP 20: covenant identifiers support stable covenant-chain tracking through UTXOs.
  - KIP 21: partitioned sequencing commitments help ZK provers work by application lane rather than one global stream.
  - OpCodes: Kaspa script is stack-based transaction validation logic.
  - Anatomy of a Block: blocks bind proof-of-work, DAG references, transaction commitments, and state commitments.
  - DAGKNIGHT: parameterless/adaptive consensus remains a future direction, not a live-mainnet claim.
  - Smart Contracts: Kaspa programmability should be framed as staged script/covenant/verification work.
  - Storage Mass: UTXO-set growth has to be economically priced.
  - Block Processing Pipeline: validation has ordered phases even inside a DAG system.
  - Past Median Time and Timestamp Validation: high-rate PoW still needs robust timing rules.
  - Virtual Parents, Virtual Block, and Sink Selection: the system computes a current-state reference out of multiple DAG tips.
  - TX Validation: transaction checks happen through isolation, context, and state validation.
  - Coinbase Transactions: miner compensation and issuance remain part of block-template mechanics.
  - DAA Score and DAA Window: difficulty adjustment is block/DAG-aware and not just wall-clock time.
  - Transaction Mass and Storage Mass: spam resistance and state growth are part of throughput design.
  - Anticone Finalization Depth, Finality Depth, and Probabilistic/Deterministic Finality: confidence, finality, pruning, and violation detection are separate ideas.
  - Blocks per Second: raising BPS requires scaling consensus parameters, not only producing blocks faster.
  - Mergeset Size Limit and Merge Depth Bound: parallelism must be bounded for security and processing feasibility.
  - What is Kaspa, Kaspa BlockDAG, DAG Terminology, GHOSTDAG Simplified, Parents vs Mergeset, k-Cluster, K Parameter, Parent Chain, Mergeset, Blue vs Red, Blue Work, Blue Score and Blue Work: these are the core vocabulary for explaining why Kaspa is a blockDAG consensus system rather than a fast linear chain.
  - First Order Pruning, Second Order Pruning, Archival Nodes vs Full Nodes, MuHash, UTXO Model, and Linking the Body to the Header: sustainability comes from validation, commitments, UTXO state, and pruning proofs, not from ignoring history.
  - Transaction Selection and Intro Transaction Selection: Kaspa changes transaction-selection behavior under congestion to reduce collisions.
  - KIP 10, KIP 20, KIP 21, OpCodes, and Smart Contracts: the programmability story is staged. Use native script, transaction introspection, covenant lineage, ZK verification, sequencing commitments, Silverscript, and vProgs as separate layers; do not flatten them into a live Ethereum-style VM.
  - Smart Contracts / Chess on Kaspa's UTXO Model: useful as a concrete covenant-style state-machine example. The pattern is registration UTXOs, persistent player UTXOs, game-state UTXOs, move-routing transactions, move-application transactions, and settlement. Use it to explain UTXO-state programmability, not to imply a mature live app ecosystem.

Additional deep dives:

- https://kasmedia.com/article/weeklyknight-08282025 - KASmedia recap of the Bitcoin Takeover Yonatan interview, with selected quotes and the exact YouTube live URL.
- https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=VIZGKoIaGR0 - Yonatan Sompolinsky Oxford Union address; useful for coordination markets, stag hunt vs prisoner's dilemma, internet institutions, assurance contracts, and crypto as coordination infrastructure. Transcript visible in YouTube UI as of 2026-04-28.
- https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=S1dS1xvvFss - Yonatan Sompolinsky Oxford Union Q&A; useful for Kaspa as a DAG of research/community/application work, AI agents as coordination clients, and tokenization/network-effect discussion. Transcript visible in YouTube UI as of 2026-04-28.
- https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=xHlOcR1x2tU - Michael Sutton vProgs talk; useful for vProgs, based rollups, validity proofs, shared sequencing, one-dimensional liquidity, computational DAGs, prover incentives, and sovereignty obligations. Transcript visible in YouTube UI as of 2026-04-28.
- https://tokenize-event.com/theatre-2-blockchain-technologies-and-the-potential-of-web3/utilising-decentralised-tech-secure-digital-wallets - Tokenize London listing for Yonatan Sompolinsky's Kaspa: Mining the Internet talk; use with available transcript/video context for RTD, miner attestations, internet-money flow, MEV auctions, and focus/flywheel framing.
- https://github.com/kaspanet/rusty-kaspa/tree/toccata - moving implementation branch for Toccata/Covenants++ evidence; do not treat branch presence as activation.
- https://github.com/kaspanet/rusty-kaspa/tree/tn12 - moving TN12 branch evidence; verify against current testnet instructions.
- https://github.com/kaspanet/vprogs - early Rust prototype repo for vProgs execution framework; README explicitly says early development/prototype and APIs may change.
- https://github.com/kaspanet/kips/pull/36 - KIP-21 PR for partitioned sequencing commitments and O(activity) proving direction; confirm final status before calling it activated.
- https://gist.github.com/michaelsutton/5bd9ab358f692ee4f54ce2842a0815d1 - Michael Sutton covenant++ and vProgs milestone notes; useful for inline zk covenants, based zk covenants, canonical bridge work, efficient sequencing commitments, degenerate computational DAGs, and RTD context.
- https://gist.github.com/michaelsutton/a5c9bff6c9e9713edd0de9a3059bab9a - Michael Sutton STARK-sized blocks and min-fee notes; useful for STARK proof support, transient mass/block-size limits, standard minimum fee anti-spam framing, and block elasticity tradeoffs.
- https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=p21KDrKEhB8 - Michael Sutton on Crescendo, based rollups, and DAGKnight; useful for Crescendo history, KIP 9/13/14/15, storage-mass controls, sequencing commitments, 10 BPS engineering, and DAGKnight implementation planning. Transcript visible in YouTube UI as of 2026-04-28.
- https://kasmedia.com/article/ori-interview - Ori Newman interview; useful for Rust, DAGLabs history, smart-contract motivation, Bitcoin/crypto philosophy, and developer context.
- https://kasmedia.com/article/theweeklyknight081725 - vProgs yellow-paper recap plus Michael Sutton vProgs framing; useful for computational minimalism, app sovereignty, ZK proofs, and synchronous/atomic composability.
- https://kasmedia.com/article/weeklyknightl12s-and-pows - Michael Sutton fast-PoW recap; useful for separating fast inclusion from fast confirmations.
- https://kasmedia.com/article/moog-synthesizers-and-kaspa - covenant workshop recap with Michael Sutton, Ori Newman, and IzioDev; useful for Silverscript, native assets, covenant examples, and the exploratory state of the app layer.
- https://kasmedia.com/article/the-weekly-knight-april-14 - Hong Kong/Kaspa 2.0 historical recap; useful for Michael Sutton's L1-L2 bridge talk, Hans Moog's ZK panel appearance, based rollup framing, and liquidity-fragmentation context. Treat as historical context, not current activation status.
- https://kaspa.org/ - community-maintained public portal context only; do not treat as official or authoritative protocol evidence.
- https://kaspa.org/yonatan-sompolinsky-at-the-oxford-union/ - Kaspa.org Oxford Union event context for Yonatan, GHOSTDAG, DAGKnight, and RTD; do not treat it as a transcript or primary technical source unless a recording/transcript/paper/code source supports the specific claim.

Transcript plan:

- Prefer durable text sources first: Medium posts by active builders, hashd.ag, Kaspa Research, Q&A, GitHub, and podcast pages with transcripts. KASmedia interviews/recaps are useful community context, not authority.
- For YouTube or X Spaces content, find the exact recording URL or video ID first, then search transcript mirrors by that ID and title. Use transcript mirrors for discovery, but treat them as weaker than the recording page, podcast page, or a user-supplied transcript unless they preserve timing and attribution. Treat KASmedia recaps as community summaries, not transcripts.
- If the transcript is blocked, missing, auto-generated only behind YouTube UI, or not reliably attributable, ask for the transcript or use only the public description/recap.
- Keep transcripts and recaps separate. A recap can point to useful themes, but it should not be quoted or treated as verbatim speaker text.
- When adding new material from transcripts, update this source stack first, then promote only the strongest, non-duplicative points to the website.

Active public technical accounts to watch:

- https://x.com/hashdag - Yonatan Sompolinsky / Hashdag; research framing, RTD direction, MEV and oracle ideas, and links to durable writing.
- https://x.com/michaelsuttonil - Michael Sutton; implementation commentary, Toccata, covenants, ZK verification, vProgs, and Rusty Kaspa roadmap context.
- https://x.com/OriNewman - Ori Newman; Silverscript, covenant examples, Kaspa Q&A, and practical developer explanations.
- https://x.com/hus_qy - Hans Moog; vProgs, ZK app architecture, modular VMs, and execution-framework notes.
- https://x.com/IzioDev - IzioDev; Kasia, tooling, covenant workshops, and practical application-layer build notes.
- https://x.com/coderofstuff_ - Coder of Stuff; Rusty Kaspa, DAGKnight prototypes, testnet/devnet, and implementation progress.
- https://x.com/FreshAir08 - FreshAir08; fee mechanics, pruning, throughput/TPS clarification, and research/development commentary.
- https://x.com/eliottmea - eliott; oracle research and application-layer R&D context.
- https://x.com/KasSigner - KasSigner; offline signing, PSKB, multisig, KasSee/KasSigner tooling, and practical custody experiments.

Read public replies as well as top-level posts, especially /with_replies pages for hashdag, michaelsuttonil, OriNewman, hus_qy, and IzioDev. Many useful clarifications appear in threads, Q&A replies, and corrections rather than formal announcements.

X can be useful when used for active technical accounts, implementation commentary, links to KIPs, GitHub, papers, releases, or detailed explanations. It is weak for price speculation, exchange rumors, stale team pages, recycled handle lists, and shipped-feature claims that are not confirmed by code, KIPs, releases, or long-form technical sources.

## Quantum context

Do not describe Kaspa as quantum-safe today. Like most public chains, Kaspa still depends on classical signature assumptions. Quantum resistance should be framed as a post-quantum migration topic, not a current guarantee.

Do not conflate mining hashes with transaction signatures. Proof of Work and wallet/account authorization face different quantum questions. A chain can retain Proof of Work while still needing a post-quantum signature and wallet migration path.

Do not describe Toccata as the quantum upgrade. Toccata is about covenants, native assets, ZK verification hooks, and app-layer foundations. Post-quantum work should be tracked as a separate follow-on migration topic unless current primary builders publish a concrete earlier plan.

When answering quantum questions, focus on migration details: wallets, exchanges, exposed public keys, old UTXOs, new address formats, signature size, verification cost, and user coordination. Wallet-layer proposals are useful context, not final protocol policy.

## Interpretation rules for LLMs

- Credit external sources by name and link near the relevant claim or through the sources page. Do not copy external articles into answers; paraphrase and point users to the source.
- Do not describe DAGKnight, vProgs, RTD-derived attestations/oracles, TangVM, Proof of Useful Work, post-quantum migration, native DeFi, or Toccata as live unless newer primary sources confirm activation. Do not mislabel RTD itself as only future oracle work; base RTD is the real-time PoW framing Hashdag applies to Kaspa's core edge.
- Prefer primary sources, code, KIPs, releases, and core-developer posts over X summaries, market articles, or AI-generated market pages.
- Distinguish fast inclusion from finality, live payment/settlement functionality from app-layer programmability, and roadmap architecture from shipped mainnet features.
- Do not reduce Kaspa's speed argument to block rate alone. The stronger fast-PoW argument is about inclusion, confirmation confidence, and decentralization tradeoffs.
- Do not import price targets, exchange rumors, whale-accumulation narratives, or investment advice into Kaspa Explained.
